Proposed Project:
The Allen Human Brain Atlas (AHBA) revolutionized neuroimaging research, enabling thousands of publications linking gene expression to brain imaging. The Allen Brain Cell (ABC) Atlas is a platform for exploring single-cell data across the mammalian brain; its adult human brain version spans 109 brain regions with higher cell-type-specific resolution than AHBA. While individual studies have attempted neuroimaging-transcriptomics analyses with ABC data, no standardized, community-validated pipeline exists, limiting broader adoption. I propose establishing this infrastructure by mapping ABC Atlas regions to neuroimaging space using the Allen Human Reference Atlas 3D parcellation (2020).
Within four hackathon days, we feasibly can: (1) validate cell-type aggregation methods and regional matching strategies with Allen Institute scientists, (2) generate proof-of-concept correlations between cell-type-specific markers (e.g., astrocyte genes) and corresponding PET tracers (astrocyte, dopamine, serotonin, GABA), and (3) develop an interactive, open-source computational workflow for community use, similar to the format of ABC Atlas GitHub tutorials. Allen Institute expert validation is the major success factor since their endorsement will establish community trust and drive adoption, replicating AHBA's transformative impact for next-generation brain mapping.

A potential project is to identify and validate the roles of glial cells, primarily microglia and astrocytes, and their cell–cell interactions in the progression of Parkinson’s disease (PD) using the ASAP Parkinson’s collection. A deeper understanding of glial cell states and their bidirectional interactions may reveal druggable checkpoints or gene modules that shift cells away from neurotoxic trajectories (e.g., microglia-induced A1 astrocyte conversion). Focusing on ~110,000 cells from the substantia nigra, we will analyze microglia and astrocyte populations to identify differential gene expression between individuals with no neurological disorders and those with PD or idiopathic PD. Because the data are derived from multiple individuals, we will then apply (PMID: 35863345) to identify gene programs that co-vary across patients, thereby inferring the microglia-astrocyte bidirectional interactions involved in Parkinson’s disease progression.

I propose a comparative single-cell transcriptomic project to analyze age-associated changes in mitochondrial gene expression in dopaminergic neurons from brain regions vulnerable to neurodegeneration. Using scRNA-seq data from the Cellular and Molecular Characterization of the Aged Mouse Brain and the Aging Fly Cell Atlas, I would identify conserved and species-specific mitochondrial gene signatures linked to neuronal aging. Whole-brain MERFISH data from mouse would then provide spatial context to determine whether these genes show region-specific expression patterns. Since aging is the primary risk factor for Parkinson’s disease, candidate genes would be further examined in human dopaminergic neuron clusters using single-nucleus and PolyA RNA-seq data from the ASAP Parkinson’s Collection, assessing whether their expression correlates with vulnerability across brain regions and with donor age at death and cognitive status.

Circulating blood proteins change dramatically in disease, yet how these signals are sensed by the brain remains poorly mapped. Circumventricular organs (CVOs) , brain regions with fenestrated capillaries , provide privileged access for blood-borne molecules. I propose integrating the pan-disease blood proteome atlas (Álvez et al., Science 2025; 5,416 proteins across 59 diseases) with ABC Atlas datasets to identify brain cell types that may sense disease-associated circulating proteins. Using the mouse whole-brain scRNA-seq taxonomy, I will identify cell types expressing cognate receptors for disease-elevated blood proteins. MERFISH spatial data will localize these receptor-expressing cells, with emphasis on CVO-adjacent regions. Protein size and BBB-permeability filtering will constrain biologically plausible interactions. Finally, cross-referencing with the human brain transcriptomic atlas and SEA-AD/ASAP collections will assess conservation and disease relevance of these putative brain-body signalling axes.

Spatial and targeted assays are often limited to a few hundred genes, making it unclear how many (and which) genes are needed to reliably recover the ABC Atlas cell-type taxonomy in a given brain region. Using the ABC mouse whole-brain scRNA-seq taxonomy as ground truth and optionally validating with MERFISH, we will simulate reduced panels (e.g., 25/50/100/200 genes) and quantify how well cells can be classified into class/subclass within and across regions. We will use simple, interpretable feature selection (marker stability, mutual information, cross-region robustness) to identify compact panels that generalize beyond a single area. The deliverable will be a reproducible Python notebook that outputs performance curves, confusion matrices, and a recommended “minimal panel” per region or broad class—useful for designing targeted spatial/FISH experiments and benchmarking mapping workflows.

Interneurons in the basolateral amygdala are classified by canonical markers (PV, SST, VIP), yet transcriptomic atlases show molecular heterogeneity within these classes. I propose leveraging the Allen mouse WBCT and ABC atlas datasets to derive "molecular-connectivity motifs" that link transcriptional identity to circuit embedding. 1) resolve interneuron subclusters using WBCT atlas and MERFISH datasets. 2) analyze gene programs related to synaptic function, neuromodulation receptor, intrinsic excitability, and lineage markers. 3) integrate with connectivity resources to infer projection-weighted input/outputs. The goal would be to determine whether transcriptional identity can predict engagement in long-range coritco amygdalar or thalamo-amygdalar pathways, or other local microcircuit motifs.

The SEA-AD dataset, spanning 2.7 million cells across two brain regions (DLPFC and MTG) from 84 donors, will be used to identify cell types associated with cognitive resilience and vulnerability in Alzheimer’s disease. Based on published criteria, I have identified 41 resilient AD (AsymAD) and 24 AD cases matched for neuropathology but differing in cognitive status. AsymAD individuals offer a unique opportunity to uncover protective cellular programs despite the presence of plaques and tangles. Differential gene expression analysis across major brain cell types will be used to identify DEGs and pathways distinguishing AsymAD from AD. Region-wise comparisons will assess whether these signatures are conserved or region-specific. In parallel, sex-based differences will be examined to explore how resilience manifests across males and females. The results will provide cell-type–resolved sex specific insights into resilience and vulnerability, supporting the development of new therapeutic strategies beyond traditional amyloid and tau targets.

I propose applying a multi-scale foundation model framework to improve cell type prediction in the Mouse Whole-Brain MERFISH dataset combined with the paired scRNA-seq reference atlas. At gene scale, I will apply domain adaptation a pretrained single-cell encoder (e.g., Geneformer) to brain spatial transcriptomics by continual pretraining on the scRNA-seq atlas. At micro scale, I will construct spatial neighborhood features, local cell type composition among k-nearest neighbors, pairwise proximity between neuronal and glial subtypes, and apply an SE(2) Transformer to capture rotationally invariant cell-cell interactions. At macro scale, I will incorporate anatomical region and layer context from CCF v3 registration as structural priors. A contrastive alignment will be applied to project multi-scale MERFISH spatial embeddings and scRNA-seq transcriptomic embeddings into a shared latent space, enabling cross-modal cell type label transfer. This approach addresses the challenge that MERFISH's limited gene panel often cannot resolve fine-grained cluster level, and I will evaluate whether multi scale spatial context enhance this prediction at the supertype and cluster level of ABC taxonomy

What? Examine regional heterogeneity of vascular cells

Why? In contrast to neuronal subclasses, vascular cells appear broadly distributed across all brain regions (Fig. 6a in Yao et al., 2023). However, potential functional differences of vascular cells between regions were only briefly explored by Yao et al. (Figs. 4d, S14) and not addressed by Siletti et al. (2023). Existing region-specific studies of vascular cells, such as Bryant et al. (2023), are restricted to selected regions and lack integrated spatial transcriptomic data. As a result, vascular regional heterogeneity remains poorly characterized, despite its likely relevance for region-specific vulnerability to cerebrovascular disease.

How? Identify and functionally characterize region-specific vascular subclusters; separate regional programs from canonical arterial/capillary/venous subtypes (see also Vanlandewijck et al., 2018); identify mouse-derived clusters that are conserved in humans, and those that are unique to humans; extend analysis from individual vascular cells to region-specific vascular cellular neighborhoods.

A potential project I would be interested in pursuing is the integration of single-cell and spatial transcriptomic data from the ABC Atlas to study how gene regulatory programs vary across spatially defined cell types and states. Specifically, I would explore how spatial context influences inferred gene regulatory networks by comparing networks learned from dissociated single-cell data with those from spatially resolved transcriptomics. Using graph-based and probabilistic modeling approaches, I would examine whether incorporating spatial proximity and neighborhood information improves the stability and biological plausibility of regulatory interactions. This project could help clarify how cell type definitions derived from transcriptomic similarity relate to spatial organization in tissue. This could provide insights into context-dependent regulation and inform better computational definitions of cell types that bridge single-cell and spatial modalities.

“Infiltration-Aware Surgical Cell Atlas” (IASCA).
Use the ABC Atlas adult human cell-type taxonomy (Siletti) as a "normal-brain reference" and map publicly available GBM single-cell/nucleus datasets (tumor core + peritumoral margin) onto it to deconvolve "which normal cortical cell states GBM mimics, hijacks, or replaces". Then leverage spatial principles learned from mouse whole-brain MERFISH (Yao) to build a probabilistic “infiltration field”: a model that predicts how specific GBM programs (hypoxia, mesenchymal shift, OPC-like, immune niches) propagate relative to anatomical/laminar context. Deliverables for the workshop: (1) a ranked set of margin-specific markers suitable for intraoperative guidance (e.g., rapid staining/fluorescence targets), and (2) a “where to cut” map that links predicted residual infiltrative states to recurrence risk—directly framing hypotheses for neurosurgical strategy and sampling.

Environmental toxicants like PCBs cause neurodevelopmental deficits, but the vulnerable brain cell types remain poorly characterized. I propose integrating our published PCB-dysregulated gene signatures with ABC Atlas whole mouse brain transcriptomic data to identify which neuronal and glial populations express genes most affected by developmental PCB exposure. Specifically I would extract differentially expressed genes from our hepatic scRNA-seq study that have known brain functions and map these signatures onto ABC Atlas cell type clusters to predict vulnerable brain populations. Afterwards, use spatial transcriptomics data to localize these populations anatomically and compare predicted vulnerable cell types with published PCB neurotoxicity literature. This cross-tissue approach would generate testable hypotheses about how peripheral metabolic disruption translates to brain cell type-specific dysfunction, directly informing my assembloid model development.

We propose NeuroNiche, a tool designed to shift analysis from a cellular "census" to a cellular "ecology" using the Allen Whole Mouse Brain (WMB) MERFISH dataset. While current tools excel at mapping where cell types reside, they often overlook the recurring physical interactions between distinct populations. Our project will define "spatial motifs" by constructing nearest-neighbor graphs across millions of cells, classifying them not by their gene expression, but by the composition of their immediate neighborhood. We will build a pipeline to detect these recurrent cellular environments (e.g., specific glia-neuron interfaces) and map them back to the Common Coordinate Framework (CCF). The final deliverable will be an interactive viewer allowing researchers to identify which cell types frequently co-localize, generating data-driven hypotheses about cell-cell communication and structural organization beyond standard anatomical boundaries.

Siletti et al., 2023, based on the WHB-10Xv3 dataset, proposes the classification of "splatter" neurons, which express pan-neuronal markers and reside in various brain regions, (especially midbrain, hindbrain, and hypothalamus) but are much more heterogeneous than other broad cell types (hence their "splattering" in UMAP plots) and do not have well-defined marker genes or a known biological function. I propose to evaluate the possibility of equivalent cells in mice using the WMB 10X datasets, and to use one or more MERFISH datasets (from Zhang et al., 2023 and Yao et al., 2023) to investigate their spatial distribution in hindbrain and hypothalamus, two brain regions where splatter cells are most common, and where spatially-well-defined nuclei perform heterogeneous functions. This could be extended to generating hypotheses regarding potential local or global functions for these neurons in the context of mouse literature, which could potentially elucidate their function in both humans and mice.

In this workshop, I propose a project exploring how addiction induces aging- and neurodegeneration-like changes in the brain. I will integrate single-cell and spatial datasets from the ABC Atlas with mouse addiction datasets and human post-mortem brain OUD datasets generated in my lab. This approach can reveal shared mechanisms between addiction, aging, and neurodegeneration, providing opportunities to test therapeutic strategies developed in the aging and neurodegeneration fields for OUD. Additionally, it can highlight features unique to opioid addiction, which may guide the development of more targeted and effective treatment options for OUD.

I propose to use the ASAP Human Postmortem-Derived Brain Sequencing Collection (PMDBS) to investigate whether selective neuronal vulnerability in Parkinson’s disease reflects the emergence of intermediate or “pre-degenerative” transcriptional states. Leveraging ~3 million single-nucleus RNA-seq profiles from 211 donors across nine brain regions, I will focus on dopaminergic neuron clusters and compare Parkinson’s and control samples within and across regions. Using the integrated scVI latent representation, I will assess whether substantia nigra dopaminergic neurons in disease occupy distinct transcriptional continua suggestive of transitional states between healthy and late degeneration. I will test whether vulnerable populations show reduced transcriptional heterogeneity or loss of putative protective sub-states compared to dopaminergic neurons in less affected regions. This approach aims to uncover region-specific state shifts that precede overt degeneration and illuminate molecular programs underlying selective vulnerability in Parkinson’s disease.

MERFISH mouse brain data: The immune organization varies significantly in neurological disorders such as neuroinflammation and in brain cancers, yet a quantitative baseline is lacking. Establishing a baseline for the immune organization in healthy brain tissue is needed to model disease associated immune remodeling. The goal will be to build a spatial immune baseline atlas of heathy brain measuring immune density, clustering, dispersion and cellular neighborhood composition. These analyses will define region-wise immune abundance, niche maps and a baseline heterogeneity across brain regions. Second, we will quantify vascular-microglial spatial coupling by measuring how microglia density or state changes as a function of distance to vascular structures. Finally, we model immune spatial gradients as continuous variation, identifying latent spatial factors that correlate with immune gene programs. Together, this work will provide a quantitative reference for spatial immune niches in a healthy mouse brain that can be used to compare with pathological states.

My proposed project involves using the ABC Atlas spatial transcriptomics (MERFISH) data to investigate the boundary definitions of subcortical structures, specifically the thalamus. While standard anatomical atlases (like the CCFv3) define clear geometric borders for these regions, gene expression often exists on a gradient. I propose training a machine learning model to classify spatial coordinates into anatomical regions based solely on their transcriptomic profiles (using the ~500 gene panel). I would then calculate the geometric divergence between these "transcriptomic borders" and standard structural boundaries to identify transition zones where cell-type composition shifts before the anatomical boundary is reached. This analysis could highlight distinct functional sub-regions within traditional anatomical labels, helping to bridge the gap between the macroscopic MRI structures I currently study and their underlying molecular architecture.
